items.question_text: What is the proper basis for airport traffic control service?

choices.text: Observed or known traffic and airport conditions.
Pilot estimates of unseen traffic whenever the pattern is busy.
Automatic assumption that pilots in the surface area cannot see one another.
Traffic management goals before local airport conditions are considered.

items.explanation: Airport traffic control service is provided based on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

items.question_text: When may preventive control service be provided?

choices.text: Only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement, with controller intervention only when corrective action is needed.
To any VFR aircraft in the pattern whenever routine approval phraseology would be inconvenient.
Only when every pilot agrees to waive routine runway-use coordination.
Whenever tower radar is available to monitor the traffic pattern.

items.explanation: Preventive control is limited to LOA-covered operations and controller intervention only when a conflict requiring corrective action develops.

items.question_text: Before ground control authorizes an aircraft or vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway, what must happen?

choices.text: Ground control must obtain approval from local control, including the runway and point or intersection involved.
The pilot or vehicle operator must simply advise that the runway looks clear.
The airport manager must first issue a temporary runway-closure notice.
No coordination is needed if the crossing will be completed quickly.

items.explanation: Ground control needs local-control approval, and the coordination must include the runway and the crossing point or intersection.

items.question_text: What must ground control tell local control about an aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff?

choices.text: That the aircraft has been taxied to an intersection for takeoff.
Only the aircraft's requested cruising altitude.
Only the pilot's estimated departure delay.
Nothing, unless the pilot asks for intersection length.

items.explanation: Ground control must notify local control of aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff.

items.question_text: What must be true before a departure starts takeoff or an arrival crosses the runway threshold?

choices.text: The runway to be used must be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel.
Only the runway centerline must be clear, even if equipment remains near the edge.
A blanket 'proceed as requested' instruction must have been issued earlier.
The airport manager must visually confirm every runway edge light is on.

items.explanation: Controllers must ensure the runway is free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a takeoff starts or an arrival crosses the threshold.

items.question_text: How should controllers describe vehicles, equipment, personnel, or other traffic near the movement area?

choices.text: In an easy-to-understand way that helps pilots recognize the traffic and its relative position.
Only by quoting the exact airport-map grid reference.
Only with clock-position references, even if another description would be clearer.
By omitting location details unless the pilot specifically asks for them.

items.explanation: Traffic should be described in clear, easy-to-understand terms that help the pilot recognize it.

items.question_text: Before issuing taxi instructions, a takeoff clearance, or movement-area authorization, what must you determine?

choices.text: The position of the aircraft, personnel, or equipment involved.
Only whether the pilot has the latest ATIS code.
Only whether the movement area lighting is at maximum intensity.
Only whether the aircraft has filed an IFR flight plan.

items.explanation: The controller must determine position before issuing those movement-related authorizations.

items.question_text: How long must a low-level wind shear or microburst statement remain on the ATIS after the last report or indication?

choices.text: 20 minutes.
Until the end of the hour.
5 minutes unless a pilot requests an update.
Only until the next departure acknowledges the advisory.

items.explanation: The ATIS must include the wind shear or microburst statement for 20 minutes after the last report or indication.

items.question_text: What is the permitted use of uncertified tower display workstations?

choices.text: Only as an aid to help visually locate aircraft or judge their spatial relationship to known points.
Providing radar services and radar traffic advisories to aircraft.
Establishing aircraft in radar contact for local-control services.
Authorizing visual separation solely by reference to the display.

items.explanation: Uncertified tower displays are only a visual aid and must not be used to provide radar services or radar contact.

items.question_text: When should you inform an aircraft of an observed abnormal condition?

choices.text: When the pilot requests it or when you deem it necessary.
Only if another aircraft also reports the same condition.
Only after the aircraft lands and clears the runway.
Only if maintenance personnel first confirm the abnormality.

items.explanation: Observed abnormalities are issued when requested by the pilot or when the controller considers it necessary.

items.question_text: What type of maneuver should not be approved in Class B, C, or D surface areas unless it is essential to the flight?

choices.text: Unusual maneuvers such as unnecessary low passes or buzz jobs.
A normal straight-in approach at pattern speed.
A routine runway crossing at taxi speed.
A pilot's request to transit the surface area at a normal speed.

items.explanation: Unusual maneuvers that are not essential to the flight should not be approved in surface areas.

items.question_text: What is ground control's role in runway scanning?

choices.text: Assist local control in visually scanning runways, especially when runways are close to other movement areas.
No role; only local control may scan runways.
Scan runways only after every landing clearance has been issued.
Scan runways only when local control specifically delegates full runway responsibility.

items.explanation: Ground control assists local control in visually scanning runways, especially where proximity increases risk.

items.question_text: What does a controller response of '(call sign) standby' mean to an aircraft calling before entering Class D airspace?

choices.text: Two-way radio communication has been established, so the pilot may enter the Class D airspace.
Communication has not been established, so the pilot must stay out until a full traffic advisory is issued.
The pilot may enter only if another aircraft confirms the frequency is working.
The pilot may enter only after acknowledging with a transponder ident.

items.explanation: A response with the call sign and 'standby' establishes two-way communications for Class D entry.

items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, what departure practice should you provide to the extent possible?

choices.text: A rolling takeoff for departures.
A full stop at the hold line for every departure before takeoff.
A mandatory return to the gate for all turbine aircraft.
A delay until all visible ash is removed from every taxi route.

items.explanation: When volcanic ash is present, aircraft should be kept moving to the extent possible and departures should be provided rolling takeoffs.

items.instruction: Is this volcanic-ash taxi directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Do not use full stops while aircraft taxi on volcanic-ash-contaminated surfaces.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the stated taxiing directive when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface.

items.question_text: If a pilot-in-command requests to return to a gate, ramp, or alternate deplaning area because of the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule, what should you do?

choices.text: Provide the requested service as soon as operationally practical, or advise that it cannot be accommodated because it would cause a significant operational disruption.
Deny the request automatically until the aircraft reaches the front of the departure sequence.
Grant the request only if the airport operator first cancels all other surface movements.
Tell the pilot to remain in place until airline management calls the tower directly.

items.explanation: The request must be accommodated as soon as practical unless doing so would create a significant disruption to operations.

items.question_text: When should ATC light signals be used to control aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel on the movement area?

choices.text: When radio communications cannot be employed.
Whenever a faster visual signal would be more convenient than radio.
Only during nighttime operations.
Only when an aircraft is already on final approach.

items.explanation: ATC light signals are used when radio communications cannot be employed.

items.question_text: What does the alternating red-and-green warning signal mean?

choices.text: General warning; exercise extreme caution.
Stop immediately and do not move until a steady green signal is received.
Return to the starting point on the airport.
Cleared to cross the runway and continue without delay.

items.explanation: The alternating red-and-green signal is a general warning signal directing extreme caution.

items.question_text: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only is airborne during daylight hours. How should it acknowledge a light signal?

choices.text: Rock the wings.
Flash the landing light continuously.
Cycle the transponder to standby and back.
Turn sharply left and then right.

items.explanation: A fixed-wing aircraft in flight during daylight acknowledges by rocking its wings.

items.question_text: If you cannot contact airport management or the operator about an unsafe landing-area condition, what must you do?

choices.text: Issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform management or the operator as soon as practicable.
Wait for the next shift and leave the runway status unchanged until management calls back.
Treat the report as unofficial and take no action unless a pilot also reports it.
Close the runway permanently until airport management sends written authorization to reopen it.

items.explanation: If management or the operator cannot be contacted, the unsafe condition must be publicized by NOTAM and passed along as soon as possible.

items.question_text: A pilot persists in requesting a takeoff or landing on a closed or unsafe runway. After you advise that a clearance cannot be issued, what may you do if the operation would not adversely affect other traffic?

choices.text: Inform the pilot that the operation will be at the pilot's own risk.
Issue a normal clearance because the pilot accepted responsibility.
Authorize the operation only if the pilot promises to remain below traffic pattern altitude.
Require the aircraft to declare an emergency before proceeding.

items.explanation: On a closed or unsafe runway, a clearance cannot be issued; if the pilot insists and other traffic is not affected, the operation is at the pilot's own risk.

items.question_text: What is the timing requirement for issuing airport condition information?

choices.text: Issue it in time for it to be useful to the pilot.
Issue it only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.
Wait and combine all condition reports into a single advisory after landing clearance.
Limit it to runway closures and omit lesser conditions unless requested.

items.explanation: Airport condition information must be issued early enough to be useful to the pilot.

items.question_text: If a braking-action report affects only part of a runway, how should you describe it to other pilots?

choices.text: Use easily understood runway portions such as the first half or last half, and include the reporting aircraft type.
Repeat the exact landmark wording even if it is difficult to recognize from the cockpit.
Issue only the Runway Condition Code and omit the pilot report.
Describe the airport building or fire station nearest the report and omit the aircraft type.

items.explanation: Partial-runway braking reports should be translated into clear runway portions and include the reporting aircraft type.

items.question_text: When must the ATIS include the statement 'Braking Action Advisories are in effect'?

choices.text: When pilot braking reports include medium, poor, or nil, or when weather conditions are conducive to rapidly changing runway conditions.
Only after the airport operator issues a full runway closure.
Only when a military aircraft requests braking data.
Whenever any precipitation is falling, regardless of runway reports or trends.

items.explanation: The ATIS statement is required when braking reports are medium or worse, or conditions suggest rapid deterioration.

items.question_text: What is the normal position of remotely controlled arresting systems during routine operations?

choices.text: Retracted or down.
Raised on the departure end unless the pilot declines it.
Raised on both ends whenever any military aircraft is in the pattern.
Any position chosen by ground control to speed departures.

items.explanation: For normal operations, arresting systems controlled by ATC remain retracted or down.

items.question_text: When must the FFM remote status sensing unit be operational?

choices.text: When the weather is below CAT II ILS minimums.
Only during CAT III autoland operations with no exceptions.
Whenever any aircraft is inside the outer marker, regardless of weather.
Only when the localizer has already been declared unreliable.

items.explanation: The FFM remote sensing unit must be operational when the weather is below CAT II ILS minimums.

items.question_text: When must appropriate airport lighting aids be provided under this emergency-lighting rule?

choices.text: Whenever you become aware that an emergency has or will occur.
Only after the pilot declares an emergency on final approach.
Only when the airport manager first requests the lights.
Only during nighttime emergencies.

items.explanation: Emergency lighting action begins whenever the controller becomes aware that an emergency has or will occur.

items.question_text: After REIL has been operating with the associated runway lights illuminated, when should it normally be turned off?

choices.text: After an arriving aircraft has landed, a departing aircraft has left the traffic pattern area, or the lights are of no further use to the pilot.
Only at the next scheduled lighting-status review.
Immediately after takeoff clearance is issued to any departure.
Only when visibility rises above 5 miles.

items.explanation: Those are the listed triggers for turning REIL off.

items.question_text: When must remotely switched VASI systems be operated?

choices.text: When they serve the runway in use, with intensity controlled by the applicable table unless a local directive or pilot need changes it.
Only when requested by an instrument-flight pilot.
Only from sunset to sunrise, regardless of runway use.
Whenever the airport rotating beacon is on.

items.explanation: VASI systems serving the runway in use are operated in accordance with the applicable intensity tables unless an exception applies.

items.question_text: What is the normal operating rule for remotely switched PAPI systems?

choices.text: Operate them when they serve the runway in use and control intensity by the applicable table unless a local directive or pilot request changes it.
Leave them off unless the runway is the airport's calm-wind runway.
Operate them only during periods of civil twilight.
Use them only when REIL is also operating.

items.explanation: PAPI systems that serve the runway in use are operated in accordance with the applicable table unless an exception applies.

items.question_text: Between sunrise and sunset, when must approach lights be operated under the daytime weather trigger?

choices.text: When the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet or prevailing visibility is 5 miles or less and approaches are being made to the applicable runway or airport.
Only when prevailing visibility falls below 1 mile.
Only when an IFR aircraft specifically requests the lights.
Whenever any aircraft is in the traffic pattern, regardless of weather.

items.explanation: The daytime approach-light trigger is ceiling below 1,000 feet or visibility 5 miles or less while approaches are being made.

items.question_text: At night, when may ALS intensity step 4 or 5 be used?

choices.text: Only when requested by a pilot.
Whenever visibility is less than 3 miles.
Whenever the local controller considers the approach busy.
Only during CAT III operations.

items.explanation: At night, step 4 or 5 is reserved for pilot request.

items.question_text: When must sequenced flashing lights be operated as part of the ALS?

choices.text: When visibility is less than 3 miles and instrument approaches are being made to the runway served by the associated ALS, or when otherwise requested or deemed necessary.
Whenever the ALS is on, regardless of visibility or approach type.
Only between sunset and sunrise.
Only when the runway is closed to departures.

items.explanation: SFL operation is tied to low-visibility instrument-approach use and may also be requested or deemed necessary.

items.question_text: How are MALSR or ODALS with separate on-off and intensity controls normally operated?

choices.text: In accordance with the applicable tables, except when local directives, pilot request, or controller judgment under the paragraph call for a different setting.
At the highest intensity whenever the tower is staffed.
Only at a single fixed intensity after sunset.
Exactly like runway edge lights with no separate exceptions.

items.explanation: MALSR and ODALS are operated by the applicable tables unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: When the ALSF-2 low-visibility trigger is no longer a factor, what system should be operated instead?

choices.text: The SSALR system.
The ALSF-2 system under the same low-visibility logic.
The runway edge lights only, with no approach-light system.
No approach lighting system at all unless a pilot declares an emergency.

items.explanation: When the ALSF-2 low-visibility condition is not a factor, the paragraph directs use of the SSALR system.

items.instruction: Which lighting action is specifically listed here?
items.question_text: Which lighting action applies once the ALSF-2 trigger condition is no longer a factor?

choices.text: Operate the SSALR system.
Keep the ALSF-2 system running solely because it was already selected.
Turn off all approach lights until visibility falls below one-half mile.
Leave only the REIL operating.

items.explanation: The paragraph directs use of the SSALR system when the ALSF-2 trigger condition is no longer a factor.

items.situation: Visibility improves above three-quarters of a mile and RVR rises above 4,000 feet, so the low-visibility trigger for ALSF-2 is no longer present. Another controller wants to keep operating ALSF-2 under that same trigger logic.
items.para_context: When the ALSF-2 trigger condition is no longer a factor, operate the SSALR system.

choices.text: Shift to operating the SSALR system because the ALSF-2 trigger condition is no longer a factor.
Continue operating ALSF-2 under the low-visibility trigger because once selected it must remain active for the rest of the shift.
Turn off all approach lighting until a pilot asks for help.
Use runway edge lights as the only visual aid because RVR is above 4,000 feet.

items.explanation: Once the ALSF-2 trigger condition is no longer present, the paragraph directs operation of the SSALR system instead.

items.question_text: What is the rule for runway edge lights when a NOTAM closing the runway is in effect?

choices.text: Do not turn on the runway edge lights for runway-use purposes while the closure NOTAM is in effect.
Turn them on automatically for every arrival and departure anyway.
Turn them on only if the pilot accepts the closed runway at their own risk.
Use them only during daylight when visibility is less than 2 miles.

items.explanation: A runway closed by NOTAM must not have its edge lights turned on for takeoff, landing, or approach use.

items.question_text: How are HIRL, runway centerline lights, and touchdown zone lights normally operated?

choices.text: In accordance with the applicable table, except where a facility directive, pilot request, or controller judgment under the paragraph changes the setting.
Always at the maximum setting once instrument approaches begin.
Only on pilot request, regardless of visibility.
Exactly the same as taxiway-light settings.

items.explanation: Those lighting systems follow the table unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: When reducing the brightness of HIRL associated with MALSR, what extra control action ensures the MALSR ends at the intended lower step?

choices.text: Rotate the brightness control below the intended step and then back to the proper step.
Turn the MALSR fully off first and then restore it to the lower setting.
Cycle the runway edge lights to standby and then back on.
Wait for the next arriving aircraft to request the change over frequency.

items.explanation: The paragraph includes a specific control-technique note for stepping HIRL with MALSR down to a lower brightness.

items.question_text: Who must be kept informed of HIRL changes that affect RVR?

choices.text: The appropriate approach controller or PAR controller, in advance if possible.
Only airport operations, because RVR is not an ATC concern.
Only the ground controller, because arrivals will see the change for themselves.
No one, unless the pilot specifically asks about the light setting.

items.explanation: HIRL changes that affect RVR must be passed to the appropriate approach or PAR controller.

items.question_text: How are medium-intensity runway lights normally operated?

choices.text: In accordance with the MIRL table unless a pilot request or controller judgment under the paragraph calls for a different setting.
At one fixed night setting whenever the tower is open.
Only during daylight when visibility is less than 2 miles.
Only when the associated MALSR is operating at step 5.

items.explanation: MIRL follow the table unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: How long should high-speed turnoff lights remain on for an arriving aircraft?

choices.text: Until the aircraft has entered a taxiway or passed the last turnoff light.
Only until the aircraft crosses the runway threshold.
Until the aircraft reaches the ramp.
Only while the aircraft is on short final.

items.explanation: High-speed turnoff lights stay on until the arriving aircraft has turned into a taxiway or passed the last light.

items.question_text: How are taxiway lights normally operated?

choices.text: In accordance with the applicable taxiway-light table unless a facility directive, pilot request, or controller judgment under the paragraph changes the setting.
Only at a single default intensity throughout the day and night.
Only when runway edge lights are at their highest setting.
Only when visibility is less than 1 mile.

items.explanation: Taxiway-light operation follows the applicable table unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: When should you turn on obstruction lights if controls are provided?

choices.text: Between sunset and sunrise.
Only when an emergency aircraft is inbound.
Only when visibility falls below VFR minima.
Only when the rotating beacon is inoperative.

items.explanation: Obstruction lights are turned on between sunset and sunrise.

items.question_text: Besides nighttime operation, when should the rotating beacon be turned on if controls are provided?

choices.text: Between sunrise and sunset when the reported ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima.
Whenever an IFR aircraft files to the airport, regardless of weather.
Only when runway edge lights are also at maximum intensity.
Whenever the airport is using a non-wind runway.

items.explanation: The rotating beacon is also turned on in daytime when reported ceiling or visibility is below basic VFR minima.

items.question_text: If a pilot reports that part of the RWSL system is illuminated and cannot accept an ATC clearance, and you verify the runway is clear but the lights remain on, what should you do?

choices.text: Turn the RWSL off and reissue the clearance.
Leave the lights on and tell the pilot to proceed anyway.
Cancel all runway operations for the rest of the shift automatically.
Ignore the report because RWSL intensity is automatic.

items.explanation: If the runway is confirmed clear but the lights remain illuminated, the lights are turned off and the clearance is reissued.

items.question_text: What takes precedence over delay or noise-abatement considerations when selecting a runway in use?

choices.text: Tailwind and crosswind considerations.
Whatever runway produces the fewest taxi delays.
The runway most pilots used on the previous hour.
The runway preferred by the approach control facility, regardless of wind.

items.explanation: Tailwind and crosswind considerations take precedence over delay, capacity, and noise-abatement considerations.

items.question_text: When may a designated STOL runway be assigned?

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot or as specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.
Whenever it shortens taxi distance, even without a pilot request or LOA.
Only for military aircraft.
Whenever wind is less than 5 knots.

items.explanation: A STOL runway may be assigned only by pilot request or under an LOA with the operator.

items.question_text: When authorizing a runway with a tailwind component, what wind information must always be stated?

choices.text: Both wind direction and velocity.
Only the tailwind component in knots.
Only the runway heading difference.
No wind information if the pilot has the ATIS.

items.explanation: When a tailwind component exists, both wind direction and velocity must always be stated.

items.question_text: When must an ASDE system without safety logic be operated continuously?

choices.text: Between sunset and sunrise.
Only when the airport is IFR.
Only after a runway incursion has occurred.
Only when all taxiways are visible from the tower.

items.explanation: An ASDE system without safety logic must be operated continuously between sunset and sunrise, and in additional listed conditions.

items.question_text: What is one approved way to identify an observed target or track on an ASDE display?

choices.text: Correlate its position with a pilot or vehicle operator position report.
Assume the nearest moving target is the one you expect to see.
Use only the target's speed readout without any external confirmation.
Identify every target by airport-map location only.

items.explanation: An ASDE target may be identified by correlation with a pilot or vehicle report, controller visual observation, or an identified ASR/CTRD target.

items.question_text: What must you not allow when there is an unidentified target or track displayed on the runway?

choices.text: An aircraft to begin its departure roll or cross the landing threshold.
A pilot to request progressive taxi instructions.
A vehicle to report its own position on the movement area.
A controller to confirm a pilot-reported position with ASDE information.

items.explanation: Departures and arrivals must not be allowed to use the runway while an unidentified ASDE target or track remains displayed on it.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: TURN RIGHT ON THE TAXIWAY YOU ARE APPROACHING.
items.display_text: TURN RIGHT ON THE TAXIWAY YOU ARE PASSING
items.explanation: The approved directional taxi phrase in 3-6-3 is 'TURN (left/right) ON THE TAXIWAY/RUNWAY YOU ARE APPROACHING.'

items.question_text: What is the required response when an arrival aircraft, still airborne before the landing threshold, activates an ASDE warning alert?

choices.text: Issue go-around instructions.
Hold the landing clearance open and wait for pilot confirmation.
Tell the pilot to continue if the runway appears clear on the display.
Transfer the aircraft to ground control because the alert is surface-related.

items.explanation: An arrival warning alert before the threshold requires a go-around instruction.

items.question_text: Which word should not be used in authorization for taxi or vehicle or personnel movement on the surface?

choices.text: Cleared.
Taxi.
Proceed.
Hold.

items.explanation: Taxi and surface-movement authorizations use taxi, proceed, or hold, not the word 'cleared.'

items.question_text: What is required each time an aircraft's taxi route crosses a runway?

choices.text: A specific runway crossing clearance for that runway.
Nothing additional if the aircraft was already cleared to taxi to the departure runway.
Only a crossing advisory after the aircraft reaches the hold short line.
A crossing clearance only for active runways, not inactive or closed ones.

items.explanation: Each runway crossing requires a specific crossing clearance.

items.question_text: What should you avoid when issuing ground clearances around heavy or super aircraft?

choices.text: Clearances that require super or heavy aircraft to use greater than normal taxiing power.
Any taxi clearance that includes a runway number.
Any cautionary advisory mentioning rotor wash or jet blast.
Any instruction for a small aircraft to follow another aircraft on a taxiway.

items.explanation: Ground operations clearances should avoid requiring super or heavy aircraft to use greater than normal taxi power.

items.instruction: Is this ground-operations directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Do not issue clearances that require super or heavy aircraft to use greater than normal taxiing power.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the stated directive in 3-7-3a.

items.question_text: How should a taxiing aircraft or vehicle normally be held clear of a runway?

choices.text: By instructing it to hold short of the runway or hold at a specified point, with traffic information as necessary.
By telling the operator to stop anywhere that appears convenient.
By issuing a warning signal only, without a hold instruction.
By transferring responsibility to the airport manager.

items.explanation: Runway proximity is protected with specific hold-short or hold-at-point instructions, plus traffic information if needed.

items.question_text: When must the ILS critical area be protected for arrivals inside the outer marker or the fix used in lieu of it?

choices.text: When the official weather is a ceiling below 800 feet or visibility below 2 miles, unless one of the listed exceptions applies.
Only during CAT III operations.
Whenever any instrument approach is in progress, regardless of weather or runway in sight.
Only if the arriving aircraft specifically requests critical-area protection.

items.explanation: The standard critical-area protection trigger is ceiling below 800 feet or visibility below 2 miles with the arrival inside the OM or equivalent fix.

items.question_text: When must the precision obstacle free zone be clear of traffic?

choices.text: When an aircraft on a vertically guided final approach is within 2 miles of the threshold and the ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than three-quarters of a mile.
Whenever an aircraft is on any final approach inside 5 miles, regardless of weather or guidance type.
Only when the aircraft is already at decision height.
Only when the runway is designated for simultaneous operations.

items.explanation: The POFZ protection trigger is a vertically guided final inside 2 miles with very low ceiling or visibility.

items.question_text: What does a 'cleared for the option' clearance allow an instructor pilot, flight examiner, or pilot to do at a towered airport?

choices.text: Make a touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full-stop landing, subject to ATC approval.
Depart and reenter the pattern at any altitude without further ATC coordination.
Ignore all traffic pattern sequencing instructions until the runway is clear.
Conduct any maneuver, including aerobatics, on the active runway.

items.explanation: The option clearance allows the listed runway operations at an operational control tower, subject to ATC approval.

items.question_text: For separation purposes, when does an aircraft cleared for touch-and-go stop being treated as an arriving aircraft?

choices.text: When it touches down.
When it enters the downwind leg.
When it reports midfield.
When it receives the touch-and-go clearance.

items.explanation: A touch-and-go aircraft remains an arriving aircraft until touchdown, and thereafter is treated as a departure.

items.question_text: What weather condition is required for simultaneous same-direction operations on parallel runways or strips, unless visual separation is applied?

choices.text: VFR conditions.
At least 1,000 feet and 3 miles regardless of separation method.
Instrument meteorological conditions with radar monitoring.
Nighttime only, with all runway lights at maximum intensity.

items.explanation: Same-direction simultaneous operations require VFR conditions unless visual separation is being applied.

items.question_text: What basic weather condition applies to simultaneous opposite-direction operations on parallel runways or strips?

choices.text: They must be conducted in VFR conditions.
They are authorized whenever both pilots have the traffic in sight.
They are authorized only when visibility is less than 3 miles.
They are authorized at night with no distance minimum if the aircraft are Category I.

items.explanation: Opposite-direction simultaneous operations require VFR conditions in addition to communication, traffic information, and spacing minima.

items.question_text: When must official ceiling and visibility be issued to a departing VFR aircraft?

choices.text: When the weather is below VFR conditions.
On every departure, even when the pilot has the ATIS and the weather is VFR.
Only when the pilot asks for special VFR.
Only when the runway is temporarily shortened.

items.explanation: For departing VFR aircraft, official ceiling and visibility are issued when the weather is below VFR conditions.

items.question_text: What should be announced to aircraft on the ground-control or flight-data frequency when gate-hold procedures are terminated?

choices.text: That gate-hold procedures are no longer in effect.
That all aircraft must shut down and await a new EDCT.
That departure control will now issue startup times.
That all pilots must request a new beacon code before taxi.

items.explanation: All aircraft on the GC or FD frequency are advised when gate-hold procedures are no longer in effect.

items.question_text: Before takeoff, what departure-control information must normally be issued to departing IFR, SVFR, VFR radar-service, and TRSA VFR aircraft?

choices.text: The appropriate departure control frequency and beacon code.
Only the departure controller's call sign.
Only a suggested initial heading.
Only a traffic advisory for the first aircraft on final.

items.explanation: Before takeoff, those departing aircraft are informed of the departure frequency and beacon code, subject to the listed SID exception.

items.question_text: What is the intent of line up and wait?

choices.text: To position an aircraft for an imminent departure when a takeoff clearance cannot yet be issued because of traffic.
To hold an aircraft on the runway indefinitely until the pilot asks for release.
To authorize any intersection crossing needed before departure.
To let the aircraft begin its takeoff roll at pilot discretion.

items.explanation: LUAW is intended to position an aircraft for an imminent departure when traffic prevents immediate takeoff clearance.

items.question_text: When may a takeoff clearance be issued before the prescribed separation already exists?

choices.text: When there is reasonable assurance the separation will exist when the aircraft starts takeoff roll.
Whenever the pilot reports ready and no traffic advisory has been requested.
Only when the pilot accepts reduced separation in writing.
Only if the departure is the last aircraft before a shift change.

items.explanation: Anticipating separation allows a takeoff clearance when there is reasonable assurance the required separation will exist at the start of takeoff roll.

items.question_text: What must you not do with a small aircraft behind a departing super or heavy aircraft when applying same-runway wake intervals?

choices.text: Do not clear the small aircraft to line up and wait on the same runway behind the departing super or heavy aircraft.
Do not issue any wake turbulence advisory under any circumstance.
Do not use runway landmarks to determine airborne spacing in daylight.
Do not clear the small aircraft for takeoff from an intersection.

items.explanation: Small aircraft may not be cleared to line up and wait behind a departing super or heavy aircraft to apply the required wake intervals.

items.question_text: How long must a Category I aircraft departing from an intersection on the same runway wait behind a departing Category G aircraft?

choices.text: At least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft took off.
At least 2 minutes after the preceding aircraft took off.
Until the preceding aircraft passes 500 feet AGL.
No interval is required if the runway is longer than 7,000 feet.

items.explanation: A Category I intersection departure behind a departing Category G aircraft requires 3 minutes.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: The hold instruction used for the required interval in 3-9-7 is 'HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.'

items.question_text: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways?

choices.text: Traffic information.
An automatic hold-short instruction regardless of runway use.
A wake turbulence waiver acknowledgment.
A requirement to line up and wait before every departure.

items.explanation: Each aircraft operating on intersecting runways must be given traffic information.

items.question_text: If the extended centerline of one runway crosses a converging runway or its extended centerline within 1 NM of either departure end, what separation rule normally applies?

choices.text: Apply the intersecting-runway or intersecting-flight-path provisions unless approved facility aids in a directive are being used.
Treat the runways as independent unless both aircraft are Category III.
Use only same-runway separation because the runways do not physically intersect.
Allow departures whenever both pilots report each other in sight.

items.explanation: Converging runways with centerlines crossing within 1 NM are handled under the intersecting-runway rule unless approved aids in a facility directive support otherwise.

items.question_text: When must the takeoff clearance include a runway crossing instruction as part of the clearance itself?

choices.text: When the airport configuration does not allow the aircraft to completely cross one runway and hold short of its departure runway before takeoff.
Whenever the pilot asks for an intersection departure.
Whenever the departure runway is temporarily shortened.
Only when the crossing runway is inactive.

items.explanation: In certain close-proximity runway configurations, the crossing runway must be stated as part of the takeoff clearance.

items.question_text: Once an aircraft has started its takeoff roll, when may a takeoff clearance be canceled?

choices.text: Only for the purpose of safety.
Whenever traffic management needs to preserve an EDCT slot.
Whenever a controller wants to resequence the departure flow.
Only if the pilot has not yet reported airborne.

items.explanation: After takeoff roll begins, cancellation is limited to safety reasons and must not be used solely for traffic-management or EDCT concerns.

items.question_text: What does a pilot's use of 'have numbers' allow you to omit from landing information?

choices.text: Runway, wind, and altimeter.
The landing clearance itself.
Any traffic pattern entry or supplementary information.
The need to verify whether the pilot has the ATIS code.

items.explanation: A pilot saying 'have numbers' allows omission of runway, wind, and altimeter, but it does not itself confirm receipt of ATIS.

items.question_text: What is one item a nonapproach control facility must forward, as appropriate, to the facility with IFR jurisdiction?

choices.text: That an arriving aircraft has been cleared for a visual approach.
The exact taxi route the aircraft is expected to use after landing.
The pilot's preferred gate assignment.
The maintenance status of the runway edge lights.

items.explanation: Forwarded approach information includes visual-approach clearances and several other items listed in the paragraph.

items.question_text: Before an arriving aircraft crosses the landing threshold on the same runway, what must exist under the basic rule if the preceding aircraft was arriving?

choices.text: The other aircraft must have landed and be clear of the runway, unless one of the listed landmark-distance exceptions applies.
The preceding aircraft only needs to report the runway in sight.
The preceding aircraft may still be rolling as long as the arriving pilot accepts wake turbulence.
The controller may always substitute LUAW traffic information for runway separation.

items.explanation: Same-runway arrival separation normally requires the preceding arrival to have landed and be clear of the runway unless a listed daylight landmark-distance exception applies.

items.question_text: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways during arrival operations?

choices.text: Traffic information.
An automatic go-around instruction any time another runway is active.
A runway-change clearance before every landing clearance.
A hold-short instruction to the arrival aircraft whether or not LAHSO is used.

items.explanation: Traffic information is required to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways.

items.question_text: If you become aware that an aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface on approach, what is the required response?

choices.text: Inform the pilot and issue appropriate control instructions or clearances, including a go-around if necessary.
Wait for the pilot to notice and correct the alignment independently.
Issue landing clearance for the intended runway and assume the pilot will sort it out.
Transfer the aircraft to ground control so they can verify the surface.

items.explanation: If the aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface, the pilot must be informed and given the necessary control instructions or clearances.

items.question_text: When may you anticipate landing separation for a succeeding aircraft in sequence?

choices.text: When you observe the aircraft positions and determine prescribed runway separation will exist when the aircraft crosses the landing threshold.
Whenever the runway is clear of departing traffic at the moment the second aircraft calls the tower.
Whenever a pilot says they are comfortable following close behind the preceding arrival.
Only when the airport is using LUAW operations.

items.explanation: Landing clearance may anticipate separation when the controller determines the required runway separation will exist at the threshold.

items.question_text: What must be included in the landing clearance when the aircraft reports at a position where it should be seen but has not been visually observed?

choices.text: Advise 'not in sight' and restate the landing runway.
Issue only the landing clearance and omit the runway number to avoid confusion.
Tell the aircraft to continue without any landing clearance.
Transfer the aircraft to approach for radar monitoring only.

items.explanation: The landing clearance must include 'not in sight' and a restatement of the runway.

items.question_text: What should you not do even if it appears a pilot has violated a regulation on approach?

choices.text: Do not withhold a landing clearance indefinitely.
Do not issue runway wind information.
Do not provide any further assistance to the pilot.
Do not tell the pilot the runway in use.

items.explanation: Apparent violations do not justify withholding a landing clearance indefinitely; the pilot might be handling an emergency.

items.question_text: When must taxi instructions be provided by the local controller after landing?

choices.text: When compliance with ATC instructions is required before the aircraft can change to ground control, or when it must enter an active runway to taxi clear of the landing runway.
After every landing, even if the aircraft can switch to ground and taxi clear normally.
Only when the aircraft requests progressive taxi.
Only when the airport is below VFR minima.

items.explanation: Local control must provide taxi instructions in those specific cases after landing.

items.question_text: Over which traffic may an altitude-restricted low approach be authorized?

choices.text: Over preceding landing or taxiing aircraft, but not over an aircraft holding in position or a departing aircraft.
Over any runway traffic as long as the low approach stays above 300 feet.
Only over aircraft holding in position because they remain stationary.
Over personnel on the runway without prior coordination if the pilot has them in sight.

items.explanation: Restricted low approaches may be authorized over preceding landing or taxiing aircraft, but not over holding or departing aircraft.

items.question_text: What determines whether a pilot request to remain in closed traffic should be approved?

choices.text: Local traffic conditions.
Whether the pilot has already been cleared for the option.
Whether the aircraft is turbojet powered.
Whether the runway has high-intensity lights.

items.explanation: Closed-traffic approval or disapproval is based on local traffic conditions.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: The approval phrase in 3-10-11 is 'LEFT/RIGHT CLOSED TRAFFIC APPROVED.'

items.question_text: At what point in the overhead maneuver is the IFR flight plan canceled?

choices.text: When the aircraft reaches the initial point on the initial approach portion of the overhead maneuver.
At the break point.
Only after touchdown.
When the aircraft reports high key.

items.explanation: An overhead maneuver is VFR, and the IFR flight plan is canceled at the initial point.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: The required overhead-maneuver report request in 3-10-12 is 'REPORT INITIAL.'

items.question_text: Before approving a military simulated flameout, ELP, or practice precautionary approach, what must be obtained from the aircraft?

choices.text: The high-key altitude or practice precautionary maneuvering altitudes, as applicable.
Only the number of touch-and-goes the pilot plans to make.
Only the destination taxi route after landing.
Only a request for opposite-direction traffic information.

items.explanation: The controller must obtain the high-key altitude or applicable maneuvering altitude before approving the approach.

items.question_text: What altitude and speed range normally define an air-taxi operation for a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft unless otherwise requested or instructed?

choices.text: Normally below 100 feet AGL and at airspeeds above 20 knots.
Normally below 20 feet AGL and slower than 10 knots.
Exactly at traffic-pattern altitude and 90 knots.
Any altitude or speed, as long as the route stays over the movement area.

items.explanation: Air-taxi is normally below 100 feet AGL and above 20 knots unless otherwise requested or instructed.

items.question_text: If a helicopter requests takeoff from a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or a location off the airport, and the operation appears reasonable, how should it be handled?

choices.text: Use 'departure from (location) will be at your own risk' instead of a normal takeoff clearance.
Issue a standard takeoff clearance because helicopter departures are always treated like runway departures.
Deny the request automatically because helicopters may only depart from active runways.
Issue a landing clearance followed by taxi instructions.

items.explanation: Reasonable departures from non-movement or off-airport locations use at-your-own-risk phraseology rather than a normal takeoff clearance.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAKE RIGHT TURN FOR NORTHBOUND DEPARTURE. CLEARED FOR TAKEOFF.
items.display_text: MAKE RIGHT TURN FOR NORTHBOUND DEPARTURE CLEARED FOR LANDING
items.explanation: The helicopter takeoff clearance in 3-11-2 ends with 'CLEARED FOR TAKEOFF,' not 'CLEARED FOR LANDING.'

items.question_text: When may a departing helicopter or powered-lift aircraft take off behind another helicopter or powered-lift aircraft?

choices.text: When the preceding departure has left the takeoff area or the preceding arrival has taxied off the landing area.
As soon as the preceding aircraft touches down.
Only after the preceding aircraft reports clear of the airport traffic area.
Whenever the pilots agree to maintain visual separation themselves.

items.explanation: Helicopter departure separation is satisfied when the preceding departure has left the takeoff area or the preceding arrival has taxied off the landing area.

items.question_text: When may an arriving helicopter or powered-lift aircraft land behind another helicopter or powered-lift aircraft?

choices.text: When the preceding arrival has come to a stop or taxied off the landing area, or the preceding departure has left the landing area.
As soon as the preceding helicopter is inside the landing area boundary.
Only after the preceding aircraft leaves the traffic pattern entirely.
Whenever the arriving pilot says the landing area is visible.

items.explanation: Helicopter arrival separation depends on the preceding arrival stopping or taxiing off, or the preceding departure leaving the landing area.

items.question_text: What minimum spacing allows simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs, assuming the courses do not conflict?

choices.text: At least 200 feet between the landing or takeoff points.
At least 500 feet between the landing or takeoff points.
At least 200 feet only if one aircraft remains below 20 knots.
No minimum spacing if both aircraft are helicopters.

items.explanation: Simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift operations require at least 200 feet between the points and nonconflicting courses.

items.question_text: If a helicopter requests landing at a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or an off-airport location, and the operation appears reasonable, how should it be handled?

choices.text: Use 'landing at (location) will be at your own risk' instead of a normal landing clearance.
Issue a standard landing clearance because helicopters may land anywhere on the airport without restriction.
Deny the request automatically unless the helicopter is military.
Tell the pilot to hover-taxi to the nearest active runway before landing.

items.explanation: Reasonable landings to non-movement or off-airport locations use at-your-own-risk phraseology rather than a normal landing clearance.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: LANDING AT NORTH RAMP WILL BE AT YOUR OWN RISK. USE CAUTION.
items.display_text: LANDING AT NORTH RAMP WILL BE AT YOUR OWN RISK USE WARNING
items.explanation: The approved off-airport landing phrase in 3-11-6 ends with 'USE CAUTION,' not 'USE WARNING.'

items.question_text: When do the sea-lane procedures in section 3-12 apply?

choices.text: Where sea lanes are established and controlled.
At any airport where floatplanes occasionally operate on nearby water.
Only when the tower is also providing approach control.
Only during daylight VFR.

items.explanation: The section applies where sea lanes are established and controlled.

items.question_text: What is one basic requirement before a departing aircraft may commence takeoff on the same sea lane behind another aircraft?

choices.text: The preceding departure must have crossed the end of the sea lane or turned to avert any conflict, unless a listed landmark-distance exception applies.
The preceding aircraft only needs to become partially airborne.
The succeeding pilot may choose the interval if both aircraft are Category I.
The succeeding aircraft must remain at idle power until the preceding aircraft reports clear of the airport area.

items.explanation: Sea-lane departures follow the same basic logic as runway departures, with listed distance exceptions where landmarks permit.

items.question_text: Before an arriving aircraft crosses the landing threshold on the same sea lane behind another arrival, what must normally exist?

choices.text: The other aircraft must have landed and taxied out of the sea lane, unless a listed daylight landmark-distance exception applies.
The other aircraft only needs to report that it is slowing down on the water.
The controller may always clear the arrival to land once the previous aircraft is past midlane.
The arrival may cross the threshold whenever visibility is more than 3 miles.

items.explanation: Sea-lane arrival separation normally requires the preceding arrival to have landed and taxied out, with listed landmark-distance exceptions in daylight.
